data.title: Mairs & Power Balanced Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Artificial intelligence (AI) and increasing market concentration took center stage again in 2025. The rate of investment in technology and AI infrastructure spending is driving the market narrative with valuations pushing higher, corporate spending accelerating, and bubble-like behaviors percolating beneath the surface.

Despite the dizzying activity, there were also encouraging signs with cooling inflation, a Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut in the fourth quarter, and continued resilience in corporate earnings. The consistency of corporate earnings is a major reason for the continued strength of the stock market. The S&P 500 is projected to deliver 12% earnings growth in 2025, which is below the beginning of the year forecast for 15% growth, but still a decent result in the face of significant policy uncertainty. Additionally, small cap companies are showing their first signs of earnings growth recovery after three years of contraction, posting 13% growth in 2025. Still, the gap between price and earnings remains wide in many places. While AI-related companies continue to command premium valuations, other sectors, particularly defensives, mid-caps, and many industrial names, remain reasonably priced. This valuation divide continues to guide much of our investment activity.

On the economic front, inflation continued to ease across the CPI, PPI, and PCE measures, landing closer to the Fed’s long-term target. Though still imperfect, these readings gave policymakers confidence to begin cutting rates in the fourth quarter, albeit with dissenting opinions. While the labor market cooled in 2025, it remains on solid footing with unemployment still below five percent.

While equity markets marched toward new highs, U.S. consumers felt mounting pressure. Rising costs, including insurance, utilities, property taxes, and groceries, continued to weigh heavily on households. In response, the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell to levels typically associated with recessionary conditions. All these data points highlight a disconnect between Wall Street optimism and Main Street anxiety.

Looking at the indices for the fourth quarter, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was 2.66% for the quarter and returned 17.88% in 2025. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (TR) was 2.19% in the quarter and 12.81% for the year, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 0.90% in the fourth quarter and 6.88% for the year.

Future Outlook

A recent McKinsey report titled, “The cost of compute: A $7 trillion race to scale data centers,” projects that nearly $7 trillion in capital expenditures will be needed worldwide by 2030 to build up the infrastructure needed to keep up with AI demand. For comparison, the cost of building out cloud computing infrastructure is estimated to be around $500 billion. One of the closest comparisons to what McKinsey is estimating would be roughly $5 trillion spent during the industrial boom, but that process took decades.

While we can’t predict all that the new year will hold for the markets, we do believe we are entering a transition period for AI. As we’ve seen with previous periods of rapid technological innovation, the initial excitement of the building-out phase is often short-lived and filled with promises (and companies) that don’t ultimately live up to expectations. Given the flood of capital into AI and the increasing use of debt and unusual financing structures, there are signals that the cycle is moving into a higher risk phase.

A helpful data point as we look at 2026 and beyond is the expectation for lower interest rates. That process began in 2024, but accelerated in the second half of 2025, and is expected to further cool in 2026. Interest rate changes, up or down, take time to work their way through the economy, but it typically takes around a year to feel the effects from changes. Also, though there has been an uptick in layoffs at large companies, it is important to remember that small businesses are the real job engine in the economy, accounting for over 80% of employment. Lower rates, and more importantly, rising small business profits, would likely support an uptick in hiring and, in turn, consumer confidence.

Finally, more stability in terms of trade and economic policy could also go a long way toward improving the consumer outlook. Thus far tariffs have not proved to be the economic disaster many predicted, largely since they have not been fully implemented. While the legal status of tariffs remains in limbo, a more stable environment would benefit confidence and, in turn, hiring and spending.

Our approach to navigating this environment remains firmly centered on identifying businesses that will harness technological advancement (e.g., AI) over the long-term, not just during the initial build-out, which we believe can increase those companies’ competitive advantages and, consequently, their growth rates. While this can mean missing out on some of the early “excitement,” identifying those companies benefiting most from these secular shifts is consistent with our philosophy and process, and we believe it is a prudent strategy for both compounding and protecting wealth over the long term.

Executive Summary

  • During 2025, the Fund underperformed its benchmark by 6.91% due to equity underperformance.
  • The most significant contributor to equity underperformance came from the Information Technology sector where unfavorable selection and a relative underweight allocation to a small handful of leading AI-oriented securities weighed on performance. In addition, Fiserv (FISV) and UnitedHealth Group (UNH) were significant detractors from performance as both companies experienced execution and management issues during the year after being long-term success stories for the Fund.
  • Roche (RHHBY) contributed positively to performance due to favorable developments in its new drug pipeline. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) also contributed positively, driven by improving loan volumes, a favorable interest rate environment, and generally stable credit conditions.
  • During 2025, the Fund added new positions in WEC Energy Group (WEC), Amazon (AMZN), and Travelers (TRV) while it trimmed existing positions in Alphabet (GOOG) and US Bank (USB). The Fund exited its positions in Honeywell (HON), Principal Financial Group (PFG), Neogen (NEOG), and Target (TGT).
  • Fixed income outperformed on credit selection and shorter maturities in the Fund which benefited from curve steepening (short-term rates down, long-term rates up).

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Balanced Fund finished the year up 6.61%. The Fund underperformed both the benchmark composite index (60% S&P 500 Total Return Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index), which was up 13.52%, and the Morningstar Moderate Allocation peer group, which rose 12.90%.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.71%.

While the Fund’s absolute performance was nearly in-line with its 20-year average, relative performance lagged the benchmark due to underperformance from the equity portion of the portfolio. The Fund’s bond portfolio produced positive total returns and outperformed the asset class benchmark.

The Information Technology sector was the largest contributor to the underperformance of equities during 2025. A small handful of companies witnessed outsized share price performance driven by enthusiasm around AI and the Fund did not have sufficient exposure to this cohort. Additionally, several technology companies the Fund has exposure to also underperformed the market. For Entegris (ENTG), margin pressure from expansionary efforts, combined with lackluster demand, helped contribute to lower earnings expectations. For Motorola Solutions (MSI), underlying results remain quite positive, but a recent acquisition is expected to dilute earnings at a time when tariff headwinds are expected to create near-term margin pressure. For Texas Instruments (TXN), the company continues to make significant long-term investments to expand capacity but in the near term has been hampered by a weak demand environment.

The Financials sector was also a significant contributor to relative underperformance due to adverse stock selection. Fiserv was a significant holding which dramatically underperformed after revising down its earnings guidance two times during the year. These revisions were due to disappointing results for its Clover product, a cloud-based payment platform which had previously been identified as a significant driver of growth in merchant acceptance. Unexpected market share losses led management to announce a strategic pivot which will result in lower near-term earnings. Offsetting the Fiserv headwind, JPMorgan & Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC) outperformed due to an improving outlook for credit conditions as well as a trend toward deregulation which we believe will benefit the largest banks. Wells Fargo further benefited as several consent decrees, which outline specific reforms and improvements, were lifted by various regulators. With these restrictions aside, the company is now in a much better position for future growth. Separately, the Fund trimmed its holdings in U.S. Bancorp (USB) due to a declining competitive advantage position which could weigh on long-term growth expectations.

The Health Care sector also weighed on relative performance due to adverse stock selection. UnitedHealth Group (UNH) lagged during the period as the company experienced higher than expected medical loss ratios which led to a significant decline in earnings expectations. Consequently, the company announced that former CEO Stephen Hemsley would return to the top role to “steady the ship.” The company has since laid out some credible steps toward improvement such as “exiting” 1 million unprofitable Medicare Advantage members while also adjusting premiums to reflect higher costs. The company still has an attractive long-term earnings profile and historically has demonstrated its ability to manage through temporary disturbances. We continue to hold our UnitedHealth position and may look to add when valuation opportunities present themselves. Bio-Techne (TECH), which makes high quality ingredients for biopharmaceutical research and production, underperformed due to uncertainty around the funding environment for biopharmaceuticals and biotech research given recent federal funding cuts and weakness in venture capital funding for biotech startups. We have taken advantage of the recent weakness to add to our Bio- Techne position. Offsetting these impacts, Eli Lilly (LLY) outperformed due to an improving outlook for its market-leading GLP-1 products while Roche (RHHBY) outperformed due to robust potential in its new product pipeline where 10 new products advanced to phase III, one step away from FDA approval.

The Consumer Discretionary sector contributed positively to relative returns. The Fund initiated a new position in Amazon (AMZN) in the spring during the market selloff associated with tariff announcements and benefited from the subsequent share price recovery. This benefit was partly offset by lagging performance from Home Depot (HD) which lagged due to tariff headwinds and sluggish housing activity.

Relative performance benefited from our lack of exposure to the Energy and Real Estate sectors, both of which lagged the market during 2025.

In the fourth quarter, the Fund initiated a position in Travelers (TRV), once a long-time holding of the Fund, as the company has proven its data and operational excellence has helped it develop a long-term competitive advantage in the insurance carrier space. The company has navigated several difficult elements in the past five years, and its improving profitability profile led to our confidence in re-establishing a position. Earlier in the year, the Fund added WEC Energy (WEC) as a high-quality utility with improving growth prospects and a best-in-class regulator.

Over the course of 2025, treasury interest rates out to 10 years fell meaningfully while 20–30-year interest rates largely stayed the same. Fixed income holdings in the Fund outperformed the index in 2025 as there are very few longer-term securities held in the Fund and the preference for corporate bonds and credit selection benefited performance. At the end of the year, several measures of interest rate volatility suggest a more muted tone in the future. The markets are notoriously horrible at predicting the future, and we give little credence to these projections, but for the sake of everyone, we hope for the lower volatility future.

Credit spreads, which were reflective of a benign credit environment at the beginning of the year, expanded with the market selloff in April but quickly retreated at the end of June and have since hovered near the lowest levels of this millennia. We have found fewer opportunities for investment lately and have generally been raising credit quality in the portfolio in small increments, most recently trimming some of our preferred bank securities which are sensitive to the credit cycle. We will continue to execute our strategy in fixed income, whereby we seek pockets of value and selectively research and buy bonds to help us achieve long-term performance.

Our equity investment process leads us to select companies with durable competitive advantages and attractive growth prospects which we believe will lead to sustainable long-term investment returns. We will use market volatility to invest at reasonable valuation levels as we did to initiate a position in Amazon. In an environment of shifting geopolitical dynamics, we have a general preference for domestic equities and domestic fixed income which we believe leaves us less exposed to emerging risks. We are, and will be, long-term believers in the United States economy and our country’s innovative prowess. While we do not make big calls on interest rates or credit cycles, we have increasingly positioned the fixed income portfolio to be optimized for a balanced portfolio allowing for effective reallocation in a market disruption. We continue to believe that our consistent investment approach to both stocks and bonds will deliver sustainable performance over the long haul.

data.title: CQQQ: A Strong Run, But No Case For Fresh Money
data.description:
The Invesco China Technology ETF (CQQQ) has had a stellar run in the past year but has not been a consistent compounder like QQQ over more than a decade. It remains sensitive to policy risks and misses out on gamechangers like Alibaba and Huawei. What is left is broadly a portfolio of consumer-facing tech platforms that are cyclical in nature. With no secular rerating trigger in sight for the top holdings and a neutral broad market setup, CQQQ does not look good for fresh longs, both for long-term investors and those looking for tactical positions.

Methodology and Implications

CQQQ is a rules-based China tech ETF that includes broad structural exposure not restricted to offshore tech names like Tencent. The market cap range is quite broad, and final weights are assigned using a modified market cap-weighted methodology, implying big names will dominate. There is no emphasis on factors, and it is best viewed as a high beta proxy of the overall Chinese tech segment (akin to QQQ for the US)—but more policy and sentiment driven.

Positions in CQQQ are usually a bet on China tech multiples expanding, accompanied by a regime where policy pressures are low and overall Chinese markets are flush with liquidity and risk appetite. On the other hand, CQQQ is expected to underperform when regulatory uncertainty rises or when earnings visibility is not strong. CQQQ (like QQQ) may work in markets where leadership is narrow but needs an overall bullish local tech environment to truly flourish.

Portfolio Walkthrough

As discussed before, CQQQ is not a tech-only portfolio from a purist's standpoint. Including communications, the exposure is still the vast majority of the portfolio (~83%). However, a significant proportion of the tech names in the portfolio include consumer-facing and platform-driven companies (not enterprise software, cloud infra, or B2B semis like in the US). As a result, returns are driven by consumer demand and engagement levels rather than by variables that influence QQQ, such as enterprise IT budgets or SaaS pricing power.

The holdings are concentrated at the top because of the market cap-based methodology. Although the portfolio reports more than 150 holdings, the top 10 account for ~55% of the portfolio by weight. Much like Nvidia in QQQ, Tencent is the real anchor with a close to a 10% weight in the portfolio. But unlike Nvidia, this is a social platform-based model with no secular tailwind. That business model difference is the flavor broadly throughout the portfolio, making CQQQ far more cyclical than QQQ, which is driven by visible earnings growth and secular tailwinds (like AI and global base). The total exposure to AI/semis in the top 10 holdings, even when the definition is broadened, amounts to just double digits (including Hua Hong, Horizon, and Sensetime). Bigger influential names like Huawei remain private—any AI tailwind there and in similar companies is not captured in CQQQ. What remains in CQQQ's tech portfolio is, on the other hand, more communications and services-oriented areas that are heavily regulated and politically sensitive. On top of missing out on private names, the ETF's tech classification net misses a strong name like Alibaba.

Use Cases

CQQQ's positioning makes it unsuitable as a core EM holding (cyclical and policy risks minus global secular tailwinds like AI) or a defensive China exposure, in my opinion. Another use case that naturally arises by definition is CQQQ as a complementary holding to QQQ, acting either as a hedge or a long-term growth enhancer.

However, the tech allocations tilt—internet platforms versus enterprise software/infrastructure—make it not a real apples-to-apples complement. CQQQ lacks an innovation-led growth in the long term and does not necessarily hedge tech drawdowns in the US because it focuses mostly on localized markets and remains sensitive to its own idiosyncrasies. Incidental periods of upside while US tech cools can happen, but there is no logical thread tying such expectations together, nor a common market geographically or by segment.

Since 2010, QQQ has grown by over 1500%, while CQQQ has grown by only 166% and through cycles. The return enhancer role is not a long-term proposition; simply looking at the historical gap in performance can best be used tactically when the outlook is supportive. For a position in CQQ (as a complement or simply rotation), investors need to have an outlook where US tech leadership weakens structurally, and China tech continues monetization highs.

The hedging or diversification angle is still in play to an extent. However, here too, correlation is positive and mostly persistent. There are very few regimes when the correlation turns negative and stays negative consistently—even accounting for what are more likely market regimes where US tech rallied, and CQQQ probably corrected (visual inspection of charts below).

Current Outlook

Since CQQQ does not seem to have a long-term strategic role to play in US tech portfolios, its tactical use case today hinges purely on the outlook for China tech (and broad macro overall). CQQQ has had a bull run in the past year with stellar returns of ~42%. The liquidity situation has been supportive. Forex reserves have been steadily improving in 2025. However, this is not as strong a long-duration support from here on, as earnings or consumer strength-led rallies, particularly given foreign direct investments (FDI) are actually sliding. FDI in 2025 (until November) was reported down by 7.5% despite incentives to attract capital. Globally, funds are still flowing into the US compared to EM or Asian counterparts. Until a sustained global interest is observed in Chinese tech and broader equities, I prefer to remain on the sidelines—especially after a strong 2025 for CQQQ.

When it comes to valuations, apart from Baidu, the other two material holdings—Tencent and PDD—do not show any broad expansion trends to go after. The good that emerges from valuations of the top 3 holdings overall is that valuations are not expensive broadly, but no secular valuation expansion trend is observed across the board to chase. The Shanghai Information & Technology sector P/E was about ~34x in late 2025, down from the ~50x highs post-COVID. This reduces drawdown risks and certainly shows tech stocks are not overheated - but also shows a lack of strong momentum that can be capitalized on. Most of the rally seen in the past year appears to be around earnings revisions after they are evident, rather than a regime where price leads earnings (like we are seeing in the US tech space). When rallies lead earnings, future growth becomes more credible in my view. The upside is therefore mostly mechanical in CQQQ, not convex, as earnings simply translate to linear share price returns.

The final variable of interest, policy risk, appears to also be neutral. The overhang of curbs on social platforms is still real in China, although the rally in the past year points indirectly to a regime where policy risks have not dragged growth.

Not a Buy Now

There are two specific times when CQQQ becomes a Buy in my view. One, a period of corrections taking CQQQ down significantly from the last highs—CQQQ's past cyclical behavior points to that being the best time to enter. We had that opportunity up until the start of 2025, when geopolitical risks and uncertainties started impacting China as much as the US. CQQQ has rallied sharply since. There could still be steam left in the rally, but a Buy now needs extra bullish confirmation now than in the 2022-24 lows.

In terms of confirmation, we have none. All we have is a neutral setup in terms of liquidity and valuation expansion momentum. Breaking down the portfolio, the overall upside in most holdings also supports my cautionary view for fresh longs. Tencent looks well poised to deliver stable earnings and cash generation, but no rerating trigger is in sight. PDD's US exposure in e-commerce is fraught with geopolitical uncertainties. Baidu looks the most promising overweight in the CQQQ portfolio, but a lot of rerating is already behind us.

Overall, in the absence of broad market rerating triggers, stock-specific action appears to be of limited impact for a high conviction Buy at this stage. Therefore, CQQQ is a hold—both for long-term investors (because of its cyclical trends) and also for tactical allocations (good run-up recently without backing foreign investments interest or broad valuation expansion).

data.title: Netflix Earnings Preview: Q4 2025
data.description:
The preview

While the market awaits the outcome of a possible Warner Brothers (WBD) acquisition, the shares are likely to remain sensitive to the news flow around a potential deal. The fight for great content and audiences by both Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount is likely an indication of YouTube’s continued growth and strength. With AI innovations coming and content creators continuing to gain viewers globally, legacy studio and media franchises may need to reimagine their business models to stay relevant. The investment of $200 million by famed investor, Tom Lee, to Beast Industries seems to be an early indication of where the future of video content is headed. YouTube channel, Mr. Beast, currently has over 460 million YouTube subscribers.

Expectations for Netflix Inc. (NFLX) into Q4 have remained stable since July 2025. Last quarter, the company highlighted the slate for Q4, but it was not a catalyst for revenue expectations. Visible Alpha consensus is expecting $12.0 billion in revenue for Q4 and $45.1 billion for FY 2025. Revenues are expected to be supported by continued new membership and monetization. Offering a range of pricing and plans combined with continuing growth in the ads business is expected to further increase monetization. Consensus expects the operating margin to increase to 24.1% in Q4 and to 29.6% for FY 2025, down 70 bps from previous expectations of 30.3% due to assumptions about higher expenses.

The company expects to grow revenues by increasing engagement trends and reducing churn while offering a more diverse entertainment offering. Gaming and the growth of ads could be key drivers in 2026. According to consensus, analysts now expect the company to generate a 32.7% margin, on expected revenue of $51.0 billion and $16.7 billion in operating profit in FY 2026.

In the spring quarter, Management highlighted that the Ad Tier enables lower prices. The Company stated in the earnings call that they expect that ads revenue will roughly double year-over-year again in FY 2025. Q4 2025 expectations for the ad-supported revenues have nearly doubled since July, suggesting increasing optimism in this emerging area of revenue generation. FY 2025 expectations have moved higher from the initial forecast of $4.0 billion back in early 2024, the expected trend has improved and is now at $5.9 billion for the full year.

Netflix remains upbeat about the long-term opportunity, given the size of its user base. Co-CEO Gregory Peters explained that 2025 will be the year that the ads business will step up to the plate to swing. Currently, consensus projects total ad-supported revenue for FY 2026 to expand to $8.7 billion, up over $2.5 billion from the expected $6.2 billion last quarter. There is a significant range of views on the magnitude of impact the ad-supported revenues will have on operating profit growth. For FY 2027, the analyst estimates for ad-supported revenue further increased to over $3 billion to $11.5 billion, but the operating profit has remained stable at $19.9 billion. The management commentary on the potential for operating profit and margin growth from the ad-supported revenues will be an important dimension to the FY 2026 outlook for the business.

Based on Visible Alpha consensus, the operating profit margin is expected to grow from 26.7% in FY 2024 to 35.0% in FY 2027. Currently, consensus estimates the operating margin now to exceed 32.7% by the end of FY 2026. However, there is significant debate among analysts with respect to FY 2026 margin estimates, which range from 31% to 35%. The debate in the market seems to be around how quickly the Company will achieve a 35% operating profit margin. This margin growth is expected to take FY 2027 expected diluted EPS from $3.94/share or 22x FY 2027 P/E, significantly lower than the 30x it traded in summer. The current consensus target price has ticked down slightly to $130 or 48% upside from the current levels.
